In this lesson, we're going to talk about selecting your niche. This is huge because the wrong niche can have a massive impact on your ability to build a big business and generate a big income. We want to make sure that you are not only nailing or identifying a viable niche or marketplace, but that you're identifying a niche or marketplace that will support the type of income that you're looking for. Now, to this day, I use a criteria in my business when selecting niches or marketplaces that I first heard years ago from a level copywriter, John Carleton. Here's what John said. He said that what you're looking for is a large, hungry mob of addicts flush with cash. I added and is accessible. I want to break down for you the different elements of this statement because there's a bunch of criteria in this one statement alone. You want to make sure that you are measuring your potential niches, your potential marketplaces against each of these criteria. The first one is that it is large. The marketplace, the niche, needs to be big enough. It needs to have enough people, enough prospects in it to support the type of income that you are looking for. If for example, you were thinking of going into a marketplace that maybe had, I don't know, 3,000 people in it, let's just say, well, you're going to have a really tough time building a six and seven figure business with a niche or marketplace that has 3,000 people in it. You're looking for a marketplace that is large enough to support a hefty income. Typically, I will tell you that in ultra tight niches, we look for a minimum of 50,000 active prospects. These are ultra tight targeted niches. For example, if you were dealing with dentists or chiropractors, for example, or podiatrists, let's just say, those are ultra tight targeted niches. You want at least a minimum of 50,000 active prospects in a niche for it to be viable. The next criteria is that the market is hungry. This means that they are hungry for the information, the solution that you are going to provide, that you're going to be offering, that they demonstrate a behavior where they're interested, they're passionate, they're hungry for what it is that you are about to offer. You don't want to go into a niche with an idea for a particular product that that niche has no interest in, that that niche hasn't displayed any behavior in consuming. You want to make sure that the niche, the marketplace, has an appetite, a strong, passionate appetite, a hunger for the information that you are going to provide. The next criteria is that it is a mob. In this regard, what we're talking about is that the niche, the marketplace, the prospects that they congregate together, that there is an event or events that they go to, that there are forums, that there are destination websites, that there are places that they gather. There are Facebook groups, there are Facebook pages, there are websites that they go to for information, for updates, for community, etc. You're not looking for a marketplace that is fragmented where they never come together. You're looking for a marketplace where they gather because this gives us the ability to advertise, to do media buying for that niche when they congregate together. The next criteria is that they're addicts. I hate this word, but it really does describe, and it really is the word that John Carleton first used. Addicts means that they can't get enough of the information. They just continue to consume more and more. A great example of this, right, wrong, or indifferent, is the internet marketing community. Internet marketers, online marketers tend to have this voracious appetite, this addiction for more information about traffic, about conversions, about generating sales, etc. Another great example of this is golfers. Golfers can't get enough of information about improving their golf game, decreasing their score. You're looking for this addiction in the marketplace. You're looking for this ongoing desire to consume more and more. The next criteria is flush with cash. This simply means that the marketplace has money and spends that money to fulfill their hunger, their quote, unquote addiction, if you will. You don't want to choose a marketplace that has no money. You don't want to choose a niche that doesn't have money to invest in their passion. For example, you don't want to go into a niche like starving artists or college students because they don't have money. They might be large, they might be hungry, they congregate, all those things as it relates to a particular topic. If they don't have money, it's going to be very difficult for you to extract premium prices and build an extremely large business. You want to make sure that the marketplace has money and regularly spends that money to fulfill their hunger and addiction. Finally, you want to make sure that the marketplace is accessible. This just means that we have a vehicle, we have an opportunity to get in front of them, to advertise to them, to market to them. Let me give you an example of a market that is not accessible. I once had a doctor as a consultant client tell me that he had an idea for an information product where he wanted to teach weekend warriors how to decrease the likelihood of getting injured. What I asked the doctor is I said, well, how are you going to access weekend warriors? Is there a trade publication that we can leverage? Is there an online forum? Is there a Facebook group? Is there a destination webpage that is specific to weekend warriors? The answer is no. The only way that we would be able to access that tight niche is if we advertise to the general fitness or athletic market. There would be a tremendous amount of waste in there because there would be a lot of individuals that aren't weekend warriors but that are regular exercisers or regular weight lifters or regular runners, et cetera. You want to make sure that the market is accessible. You want to make sure that they are searching online, for example. You can use a free keyword tool. You can use a paid keyword tool to find out if the market is actually searching online. If they are searching online, then that means that we can access them through various search engine advertising, Google AdWords, for example, or Bing. You want to make sure that the market is accessible. Again, what you're looking for is a large, hungry mob of addicts that are flush with cash and that are accessible. Let me give you six basic questions that you can ask yourself as you are brainstorming looking at dissecting various niches to determine whether they are viable and whether they meet this criteria. The first one, of course, is this market large enough to support my business? Is there that minimum of 50,000 active prospects in the market? Next is, does this market display an existing appetite for the types of products and information that I want to provide? Next is, of course, does this market congregate somewhere? Do they gather? Do they gather in forums, blogs, websites, events, and so on? Does this market consistently invest in their hunger for more? Can you see that there are other advertisers that are regularly, consistently, and frequently advertising similar products and services to this market? If there are competitors, if there are other marketers, well, that's a good sign. If there are no marketers advertising to this niche, it's not a good sign because that is a good indication that these individuals are not buyers, are not buying, and are not regularly investing in their hunger for more. Does this market have money? Are you dealing with a marketplace that is able to invest in the fulfillment of their desires? Of course, can you access this market online? Now, there are a couple of additional factors that I want you to consider when thinking about the niche or marketplace that you're going to enter into. One is affinity, meaning do you have any kind of existing relationship, knowledge, understanding of the marketplace? It's better for you to choose a niche that you have some level of affinity with so that you can better understand what the marketplace wants, their problems, their desires, the most desirable solutions, etc. As well, when you have a certain level of affinity with the market, you understand not only how the market thinks, but you understand how the market communicates. This will certainly help with your ability to effectively market to the niche. Next is gender. Is the niche made up of mainly males? Obviously, it goes without saying that males and females think differently. They communicate differently. They have different desires. For example, there's a vast difference between the massage therapist niche and the chiropractic niche. The chiropractic niche is male dominated. The massage therapist niche is female dominated. Going into a female dominated marketplace requires that you're able to understand what the marketplace requires, that you're able to communicate in a way that resonates with females and vice versa, something to consider. Trends. Meaning, is this marketplace growing? Is this marketplace shrinking? Are there legal issues? Are there trends in the marketplace that are making it less likely that new individuals are going to go into it? Are there trends taking place in the marketplace that makes it more likely that individuals in the market or niche are going to exit the marketplace? You want to understand if there's something going on in the marketplace that is going to play a role in what that market looks like in the near and distant future. The next factor to consider is psychographics. This is, what does the marketplace think and feel as it relates to the type of information and product or service that you're going to provide? Do they have negative feelings? Are they tainted? Have they had negative experiences in the past? Are they very open? Are they very excited and hopeful about the type of information or product or service that you are going to provide? This is important for you to understand because again, this plays a role in how you're going to have to communicate with that market. The next factor to consider is what's called marketplace churn or turnover. This is how often are new people coming into the market and how often are people in the market leaving? For example, there are certain career niches or professional niches where turnover is extremely high, meaning people are constantly leaving and new people are constantly coming in. For example, the personal trainer niche. That's a marketplace where it doesn't take that much to get into that niche, to be become a professional trainer and therefore it's a lot easier for people to leave. You see individuals coming into that niche or into that career and you see individuals that are leaving that career. Whereas let's say for example dentists, because of the difficulty of becoming a dentist, there's a lot less churn in that niche compared to the personal trainer niche. Churn is a good thing. It's a good thing when there are constantly new individuals coming into the marketplace because it allows you to market to new individuals in the marketplace. Next factor is price sensitivity. Is the market sensitive to higher prices? You ultimately want to choose a niche or marketplace that is not sensitive to premium pricing. Obviously, if they are sensitive to premium pricing, it impedes your ability to charge premium prices. A great example of this is the difference between the massage therapist niche and the chiropractic niche. Massage therapist niche has much greater price sensitivity compared to chiropractors. A big part of that is because of the value of a client to a massage therapist is far less than the value of a patient to a chiropractor. The point is that they are a lot more sensitive. Your ability to charge premium prices with massage therapists is a lot lower than your ability to charge premium prices with chiropractors. That's something to consider. The next factor is any kind of regulatory or legality issues. For example, when you're dealing with the medical community, when you're dealing with doctors, when you're dealing with chiropractors, physical therapists, there are certain things that you can and cannot say. There are certain things that you can say in your marketing, certain things that you can't say in your marketing. There are certain things that those professional niches can and can't say. You want to have an understanding of are you going into a niche with various regulatory and legality issues. I recommend that you stay away from the niches where there are these kind of issues because it brings up a whole other element to your marketing. It requires a whole additional element of your business to make sure that you're falling within the regulatory legality issues. Another great example of this is the vitamin supplement arena. Anytime a consumer is consuming something, there are certain regulatory issues, certain things that you can and cannot say and you need to be aware of those. The next factor is seasonality. That means that is this a seasonal marketplace? Is this the kind of marketplace that is active for certain months, certain time throughout the year? Or is this a marketplace that is just active every day, seven days a week, 24 hours a day? What is the seasonality factors or are there any seasonality factors? Then finally, the last factor to consider is the potential for renewable or continuity income. Does the market provide you with an opportunity to plug customers into a membership, into a newsletter, into an ongoing billing program? Is this marketplace going to allow you to plug people into continuity? Or is there a lifespan of the customer in that niche? In other words, for example, let's say a realtor, for example, who works with clients to help them find a home. Well, there's not really an opportunity for renewable or continuity income. You help a client find a home and then you are done working with them. Ultimately, you want to find niches or markets that give you the ability to have a continuity type of program in place. These are the additional factors to consider when brainstorming ideas for different niches and marketplaces that you want to go into.
